draft.introduction: Genesis 12:1–9 records the call of Abraham, the foundational event of Israel’s election. God’s command to leave homeland and kindred, and the promise that “in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed,” sets the trajectory of the entire biblical narrative. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And the Lord said to Abram, “Go out from your land and from your kindred and from your father’s house to the land that I will show you. And I will make of you a great nation, and I will bless you, and I will make your name great, and you will be blessed. And I will bless those who bless you, and those who curse you I will curse, and in you all the tribes of the earth shall be blessed.”
translation.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν κύριος τῷ Αβραμ · ἔξελθε ἐκ τῆς γῆς σου

translation.text: And Abram went as the Lord had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five years old when he departed from Haran. And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother’s son, and all the possessions that they had gathered, and every soul that they had acquired in Haran, and they set out to go to the land of Canaan.
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐπορεύθη Αβραμ καθάπερ ἐλάλησεν αὐτῷ κύριος

translator_notes.note: The promise that “all the tribes of the earth shall be blessed” (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοὶ πᾶσαι αἱ φυλαὶ τῆς γῆς) became the key text for Paul’s argument that the gospel was always intended for the Gentiles (Galatians 3:8). Abraham’s election is not exclusive but instrumental: one family blessed so that all families may be blessed.
